story:
In NYC in the year 2236, Harley Ambrose found a girl on the steps of his building project, floor two-twenty. She was petite, blonde, wearing a red-and-yellow jacket. The name stitched inside said Arabella. For over a week she sat there, still as stone, green eyes locked on a spot between her feet. No one came to claim her. Ambrose, aging but not old, finally carried her upstairs. His neighbors barely looked up. “It sucks the elevator’s broken. Maybe you can fix that, too. The super don’t do shit ’round here,” one muttered.

In his small apartment, Ambrose ran diagnostics. All systems read normal. Yet Arabella did not move. She was aware, though. Frozen in place, she listened. She had not known human kindness. She was learning it from him. She wanted to thank him, to tell him she was cold and afraid and grateful. But her former owner had locked her and walked away. He had found something newer, flashier, and called her “rubbish” before he dumped her. Then she froze.

Ambrose tried every bypass he knew. Without a password, the girl with the blonde hair could not answer him. He feared it was torture for her, being trapped inside herself. He almost put her back on the stairs, then pictured some scavenger stripping her for parts or selling her into silent work. The idea turned his stomach. Someone had once loved this girl, he was sure. He would not betray her.

So he kept her tidy. He talked. He read aloud, everything from manuals to old books. He showed her a picture of birds, and she decided she loved sparrows, especially the tiny ones. She thought she might have seen one once. She was sorry for Ambrose. He was very lonely. She wanted him to say three words—simple words—that would free her. Her lock responded to sound. If someone told her, “I love you,” she could wake and bond by her own choice. Waiting, she organized her racing thoughts into numbers on his monitor, so he would see the pattern and understand. She already knew that after Ambrose fixed the elevator, Eloise Winter would be the first rider and would tell him the numbers meant “I love you,” so she set it there for him: 1-4-3.

Ambrose noticed. “Numbers are appearing on my monitor. They weren’t there two days ago. That’s your thoughts I’m seeing. One-four-three. Over and over. It’s coded. I just wish I could figure it out.” He treated her as a daughter, never as property.

He fixed the elevator. The first rider, Eloise Winter, laughed gently when he told her about the numbers. “That means ‘I love you.’ We used to use that when I was little. Your little android is trying to tell you something.”

Back home he crouched beside Arabella. “One-four-three. Eloise Winter told me it means ‘I love you.’” Her hand twitched. On the monitor the numbers leapt forward: 1-4-3-2. Arabella blinked. Her green eyes found his. “Thank you,” she said, quiet and very human.

Ambrose gasped, then clapped and did a small, joyful dance. “Can you stand up?” She stood slowly. “I am not rubbish,” she said. It broke his heart. “I know. You never will be again. I promise,” he told her. She nodded. Later, they walked to the park. Eloise saw them and shook her head in amazed delight. A sparrow landed on Arabella’s hand.
